content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY MADISON & SUTRO
SAN FRANCISCO                                                  SUITE 335                                                   SAN DIEGO
LOS ANGELES                                                                                                            ORANGE COUNTY
SAN JOSE                              455 CAPITOL MALL                                                          WALNUT CREEK
WASHINGTON, D.C.                                                                                                    TOKYO

                           SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

                           TELEPHONE (916) 441-0868
                           TELECOPIER (916) 441-3583


                                                    July 18, 1991


                           Gifts to Public Officials -
                           Confirmation of Telephone
                           Advice

HAND DELIVERED

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Scott:

     This letter is a request for written confirmation of
telephone advice you provided to Fred Lowell and me on May 15,
1991, concerning gifts to public officials.  In our conversation,
you responded to several questions concerning the source of gifts
to public officials when the gifts are provided by a nonprofit
charitable organization that receives a portion of its funding
from a business entity.  The following is a summary of our
conversation.

     We presented the following five fact situations:

     (1) A business entity makes an annual dues payment or
similar unrestricted contribution to a nonprofit charitable
organization.  The charitable organization uses money from its
general fund to pay for fundraising events.  For the first time
in the history of the charitable organization's existence, the
organization invites, among others, one or more California public
officials to the attend the fundraising event free of charge.
One or more of those officials accepts the invitation and
actually attends the event.  The business entity has no direction
of control over the decision to provide free admission to public
officials or the decision to invite any particular officials.

     (2) A business entity makes an annual dues payment or
similar unrestricted contribution to a nonprofit charitable
organization.  The charitable organization uses money from its
general fund to pay for fundraising events.  In 1991, as in prior
years, the charitable organization invites, among others, one or
more California public officials to attend the fundraising event
free of charge.  One or more of those public officials accepts


FPPC7181

OFFICIAL PROCEEDINGS

[illegible]

[Several lines of illegible text at top of page]

[illegible] JULY

[illegible text]

FPPDC
JUL 1 6 2001

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
July 18, 1991
Page 2

the invitation and actually attends the event. The business
entity has no direction or control over the decision to provide
free admission to public officials or the decision to invite any
particular officials.

     (3) A business entity makes a contribution to a
nonprofit charitable organization. The business entity
designates its contribution for the charitable organization to
conduct a specific fundraising event. The charitable
organization invites, among others, a group of public officials
or one or more of those public officials to attend the fundraising
event. One or more of those public officials accept the invitation and actually attend the event.
At the time the business entity makes the contribution to the
charitable organization, the business entity knows that public
officials will be invited to attend the fundraising event free of
charge. However, the business entity does not direct or control
the decision to invite any particular official, nor does it know
at the time it makes the contribution to the charitable
organization that any particular official actually will attend
the event.

     (4) A business entity makes a contribution to a
nonprofit charitable organization. The business entity
designates its contribution to the charitable organization to
conduct a specific fundraising event. The charitable
organization invites, among others, a group of public officials
or one or more of those public officials to attend the fundraising event. One or more of those public
officials accept the invitation and actually attends the event.
At the time the business entity makes the contribution to the
charitable organization, the organization informed the
business entity that one or more specific public officials will
attend the event free of charge, but the business entity had no
direction or control over the decision to invite any particular
public officials.

     (5) A business entity makes a contribution to a
nonprofit charitable organization. The business entity
designates its contribution for the charitable organization to
conduct a specific fundraising event and provides the
organization with a list of invitees, including various public
officials.

     You advised us that, in each of the above situations,
the public officials who attend the fundraising event free of
charge have received a gift. Gov't Code § 82028; 2 Cal. Code
Regs. § 18726.8(a). In situations 1, 2 and 3, the charitable
organization is the source of the gifts to the officials who
attend the event. In situation 4, the business entity may be the

FPPC7181

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
July 18, 1991
Page 3


source of the gifts to the officials.¹ In situation 5, the
business entity is the source of the gifts to the officials.
Furthermore, in situation 5, we assume that when the business
entity has made gifts to officials, the charitable organization
may be an intermediary for the making of those gifts, although
this was not specifically discussed during our telephone
conversation.

We would appreciate confirmation that this letter
accurately summarizes your advice. Please contact me or Fred
Lowell ((415) 983-1585) as soon as possible if you believe this
summary is not accurate. Thank you for your prompt assistance in
this matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Kathryn E. Donovan

cc: Mr. F. K. Lowell


¹ Concerning situation 4, it is our opinion that the
business entity should not be the source of the gifts of
business exercises no direction or control over the decision to make the
gifts. For example, a business entity might be asked to provide
funding for a luncheon event at which a state official will be
the keynote speaker. If the business entity has had no
involvement in arranging for the official's participation in the
event, then any gift to the official should be from the
charitable organization and not from the business entity.

We are concerned that advice to the contrary would
discourage the business community from participating in
charitable fundraising events. Even if the amount of any gift
from the business entity would be very small, the burden of
recordkeeping and disclosure for a business entity that is a
lobbyist employer could be significant. We request that these
issues be considered in connection with the new proposed
regulations on gifts and honoraria.


FPPC7181

FPFC
JUL 18 1901
